claims.claim_text: A precise December 2012 late-night scene at Stanford is presented as the moment Geoffrey Hinton felt decades of work were vindicated.
claims.anchor_text: It was December 2012, a chilly night at Stanford University at precisely 11:47 PM, when Geoffrey Hinton realized everything he'd fought for over forty years was finally vindicated.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 2012, AlexNet's ImageNet victory was widely seen as vindication for Geoffrey Hinton's long-running work on neural networks.
claims.rationale: The cinematic timestamp, location, and internal realization are too specific to leave unattributed.

claims.claim_text: AlexNet won the 2012 ImageNet competition by roughly 11 percentage points over the runner-up.
claims.rationale: The CACM version of the AlexNet paper directly gives the 15.3% vs 26.2% ILSVRC-2012 result.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes an exact exhaustion-era quote to Hinton about being called crazy and neural nets being a dead end.
claims.anchor_text: "They called us crazy for years," Hinton said quietly, his British accent thickened by exhaustion. "Said neural nets were a dead end. Said we were wasting our careers."
claims.suggested_rewrite: For years, many researchers treated neural networks as a less promising path than symbolic or statistical approaches.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed verbatim quote and should not remain as a sourced-looking anecdote.

claims.claim_text: Hinton is described as having worked on neural networks since 1972 through ridicule, funding denials, and two AI winters.
claims.anchor_text: He'd been working on neural networks since 1972—four decades of ridicule, funding denials, and watching colleagues abandon the field.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By the 2010s, Hinton had spent decades working on neural networks through periods when the approach was out of favor.
claims.rationale: The exact start year and surrounding hardship details are presented too precisely without attribution.

claims.claim_text: Google is said to have acquired Hinton's company within weeks for $44 million.
claims.anchor_text: Within weeks, Google would acquire Hinton's company for $44 million.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Soon afterward, Google acquired Hinton's startup DNNresearch.
claims.rationale: The exact dollar figure is a fragile press-report detail and should be removed unless a solid source is supplied.

claims.claim_text: The module presents an exact 2013 Hinton quote about the wilderness years.
claims.anchor_text: > "We were in the wilderness for decades. Then, almost overnight, we weren't."
claims.suggested_rewrite: > Hinton later described the pre-2012 period as years when neural-network research was far from the mainstream.
claims.rationale: This is an exact quotation with no reliable interview source identified in-module.

claims.claim_text: In 1943, Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts published "A Logical Calculus of the Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity" and showed that networks of simple units could compute logical functions.
claims.rationale: The cited page directly identifies the 1943 paper and its logical-function claim.

claims.claim_text: Walter Pitts is described as self-taught, having run away from home at 15, taught himself logic, and impressed Bertrand Russell.
claims.span_hint: section: The First Artificial Neuron (1943) / Did You Know
claims.rationale: Wikipedia's Walter Pitts entry directly covers these biographical details.

claims.claim_text: In 1950, Alan Turing published "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" and proposed the imitation-game test later called the Turing Test.
claims.rationale: The primary source is real and exact, but Oxford Academic is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Turing predicted that by 2000 machines could fool 30% of judges in five-minute conversations.
claims.rationale: This specific forecast belongs to the primary Turing paper, which is currently off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Turing wrote Turochamp before suitable computers existed, executed it by hand, and it played weak but legal chess.
claims.rationale: The Turochamp page directly covers the hand execution and early-game history.

claims.claim_text: The 1955 Dartmouth proposal by McCarthy, Minsky, Rochester, and Shannon coined the term "Artificial Intelligence."
claims.rationale: The original proposal is the right source, but its host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Frank Rosenblatt invented the perceptron in 1957 as a system that could learn from examples.
claims.rationale: The perceptron page directly covers Rosenblatt, the 1957 date, and its learning setup.

claims.claim_text: The module quotes a specific New York Times headline and sensational forecast about the perceptron becoming conscious.
claims.anchor_text: The New York Times headline read: **"NEW NAVY DEVICE LEARNS BY DOING"** and predicted the machine would eventually "walk, talk, see, write, reproduce itself and be conscious of its existence."
claims.suggested_rewrite: Press coverage of the perceptron was highly sensational and overstated what the system could eventually do.
claims.rationale: The lesson about hype can remain, but the exact headline and quote should not stay without a precise source.

claims.claim_text: Logic Theorist proved 38 of the 52 theorems in Chapter 2 of Principia Mathematica, and one proof was more elegant than Russell's.
claims.span_hint: section: Symbolic AI: Logic and Search
claims.rationale: The Logic Theorist page directly discusses the theorem counts and the elegance anecdote.

claims.claim_text: ELIZA was created in 1966 by Joseph Weizenbaum, and people formed emotional attachments to it, including Weizenbaum's secretary.
claims.span_hint: section: Symbolic AI: Logic and Search
claims.rationale: The ELIZA page directly covers both the 1966 origin and the well-known attachment anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The Loebner Prize ran from 1991 to 2019 as an annual Turing Test-style chatbot competition.
claims.span_hint: section: Symbolic AI: Logic and Search / Did You Know
claims.rationale: The Loebner Prize page directly gives the date range and purpose.

claims.claim_text: Minsky and Papert's 1969 book Perceptrons showed that single-layer perceptrons cannot learn XOR.
claims.rationale: The perceptron history page directly discusses the XOR limitation and the 1969 critique.

claims.claim_text: MYCIN diagnosed bacterial infections and recommended antibiotics, performing strongly within its narrow domain.
claims.rationale: The MYCIN page directly covers its clinical scope and historical evaluations.

claims.claim_text: The module presents Hinton's exact label for the AI-winter period as "the wilderness years."
claims.span_hint: section: What Kept Going / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: He called this period "the wilderness years."
claims.suggested_rewrite: He continued working on neural networks even while the field was out of fashion.
claims.rationale: This exact quotation needs attribution or removal.

claims.claim_text: IBM's Deep Blue defeated Garry Kasparov in a six-game match in 1997.
claims.rationale: IBM's history page directly states the 1997 match result.

claims.claim_text: Hochreiter and Schmidhuber introduced LSTM in 1997 to address the vanishing-gradient problem in recurrent neural networks.
claims.rationale: The LSTM page directly states the 1997 origin and the vanishing-gradient motivation.

claims.claim_text: ImageNet is described with exact counts of 14 million labeled images and 21,841 categories.
claims.anchor_text: Fei-Fei Li's team at Stanford created ImageNet, a database of 14 million labeled images across 21,841 categories:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Fei-Fei Li's team at Stanford created ImageNet, a massive labeled image dataset that became a key benchmark for computer vision:
claims.rationale: The exact totals vary by ImageNet version and should be removed unless a precise edition-specific source is used.

claims.claim_text: Hinton's 2006 deep belief net paper introduced layer-wise pretraining for deep networks.
claims.rationale: The paper is the correct source, but MIT Press is off the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that nearly everyone in computer vision switched to deep learning within two years of AlexNet.
claims.anchor_text: Within two years, nearly everyone in computer vision had switched to deep learning.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Within a few years, deep learning became the dominant approach in computer vision research.
claims.rationale: The adoption claim is directionally true but too absolute and unsourced as written.

claims.claim_text: The 2017 paper "Attention Is All You Need" introduced the Transformer architecture based on self-attention without recurrence.
claims.rationale: The arXiv abstract directly describes the Transformer and its no-recurrence design.

claims.claim_text: BERT was introduced in October 2018 as bidirectional Transformer pretraining for language understanding.
claims.span_hint: section: BERT and GPT: Language Transformers (2018)
claims.rationale: The arXiv page directly gives the submission date, title, and bidirectional pretraining description.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI's GPT was introduced in June 2018 as a generative pre-trained Transformer using autoregressive next-token prediction.
claims.span_hint: section: BERT and GPT: Language Transformers (2018)
claims.rationale: OpenAI's 2018 post directly presents GPT as Transformer-based unsupervised pretraining.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI initially withheld the full GPT-2 release because of misuse concerns.
claims.rationale: OpenAI's GPT-2 announcement explicitly says the full model was not released because of malicious-use concerns.

claims.claim_text: GPT-3 was introduced in 2020 and demonstrated strong few-shot learning behavior.
claims.rationale: OpenAI's GPT-3 page directly emphasizes few-shot performance and the 2020 release.

claims.claim_text: ChatGPT reached 100 million users in two months and was the fastest-growing consumer application at that time.
claims.rationale: OpenAI's 2025 policy post explicitly states the two-month, 100-million-user milestone.

claims.claim_text: The module says Jensen Huang recognized the GPU opportunity early and that NVIDIA became the most valuable chipmaker in the world.
claims.span_hint: section: The AlexNet Earthquake (2012) / Did You Know
claims.anchor_text: NVIDIA's Jensen Huang recognized this early and pivoted the company toward AI, making NVIDIA the most valuable chipmaker in the world.
claims.suggested_rewrite: NVIDIA's GPUs became central to modern AI workloads, and the company later emerged as a dominant AI hardware vendor.
claims.rationale: The superlative corporate-finance claim is time-sensitive and unnecessary to the teaching point.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that gpt-5 passes the bar exam but cannot reliably count words.
claims.anchor_text: gpt-5 passes the bar exam but can't reliably count words
claims.suggested_rewrite: modern language models can perform impressively on some benchmarks while still failing on simple tasks.
claims.rationale: This is a model-specific, time-sensitive benchmark claim that should not remain unsourced.

claims.claim_text: Richard Sutton published "The Bitter Lesson" in March 2019 and argued that general methods leveraging computation ultimately win by a large margin.
claims.rationale: The essay itself is the correct source, but its host is off-allowlist.

further_reading.title: AlexNet (CACM reprint)
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the cleanest allowlisted source for the 2012 ImageNet breakthrough that anchors the modern deep-learning era.

further_reading.title: Attention Is All You Need
further_reading.why_relevant: This paper marks the Transformer shift that connects earlier AI history to present-day language models.

notes: Excluded quiz items, code blocks, diagrams, and tables per instructions. The opening scene and several Did You Know quotes are the main unsalvageable spots: they read like sourced historical anecdotes but currently rely on unattributed specificity.
